artifacts.content:
Hi Jake,

Catching a quiet half hour between rides and figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you random photos of Juniper covered in mud.

Things here are…busy, as usual. Winter hit the hay bill hard, and I’m still chipping away at that CareCredit line from when Copper colicked at the CSU Veterinary Teaching Hospital back in October 2020. I think I’ve finally got the balance under $3k, which feels like progress. There are definitely months where the minimum payment plus winter hay means I push my own stuff off a bit, but I’m trying to be smarter about that.

Speaking of my own stuff, my back has been a bigger topic lately. I’ve been seeing Dr. Hannah Leclerc, the sports med doc at UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital here in Longmont, since February for the lower back pain and that weird numbness down my right leg. At my April 10, 2024 appointment she brought up the possibility of a lumbar microdiscectomy “down the road” if PT and the injections stop doing their job. Not exactly what I wanted to hear, but also not an emergency. I’m doing the PT religiously and trying to avoid getting launched by anything that thinks it’s in the NFR.

I still think about that comment you made at Mom and Dad’s in Fort Collins in May 2023 — “You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever.” I know it came from a place of worry, but it stuck more than I let on. I am trying to build something sustainable here, even if it doesn’t look like your engineering career path.

On the plus side, the new leased place northeast of Longmont is working out well. The 50-foot round pen drains better than the old arena at High Plains, and having that smaller 20x20 catch pen attached to the shelter has been a lifesaver for the hotter mustangs.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How’s work? Still buried in CAD models and broken prototypes? Let me know when you’re free for dinner at Mom and Dad’s — I’ll bring dessert if you promise not to critique my life choices at the table this time.

Love you,
Em

Hi Laura,

Finally sitting down after evening feed to answer your last message properly.

First, thank you again for that monster of a reference letter and for generally talking me off ledges when I spiral. I keep reminding myself that you’ve watched me ride 4–6 days a week since 2018 and would probably tell me if I’d suddenly turned incompetent.

The anxiety piece has been…better, mostly. Since November 2023 I’ve been going to that small women-only anxiety support group that Megan Ortiz, LCSW, runs at the Longmont Community Counseling Center. It’s weirdly comforting to hear other people talk about panic attacks that show up out of nowhere. Megan keeps circling us back to those trailer-loading wrecks in 2020 and 2022 that I can’t seem to forget.

You know that multi-horse trailer rollover on County Road 49 in Weld County last December (2023) that I helped with? I thought I was fine afterward, but in January I ended up sitting in the parking lot at the Longmont Humane Society, shaking, and emailing Sarah Jensen from my phone. I literally typed, “Is it possible to withdraw late if my mustang comes in hotter than expected? I’m still a little shaken from that trailer wreck I helped with in Weld County last winter.” She wrote back kindly, but I still felt like such a flake.

On the flip side, Megan says I’m managing things better now — like after that trailer-loading scare last fall, I actually used the breathing stuff instead of just white-knuckling through it. It helps to remember that my “calm” around horses is something I practice, not magic.

Anyway, enough brain talk. I can haul in to your place next Tuesday or Thursday to use the arena — whichever works better around your lesson blocks. I’ve got a sensitive bay gelding who needs to see a busier environment but does not need High Plains drama.

Text me when you have a minute.

Em

metadata.subject: Re: next week + my overthinking brain

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just wanted to check in after your appointment yesterday and make sure you’re not overdoing it.

I put “Fort Collins – UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic with Dr. Natarajan” into my calendar for next Thursday again so I remember to block out midday lessons. It still blows my mind that it’s already been since August 2023 that she officially called it rheumatoid arthritis. You sounded tired on the phone after the methotrexate labs, but not as wiped as after those first visits.

Please don’t feel bad about me leaving a lesson or two early now and then to drive you up there. Most of my clients are understanding when I explain I’m taking you to UCHealth for RA monitoring, and the ones who aren’t…well, they can find someone else. Horses are important, but you’re more important.

My back stuff is still in the mix too. I saw Dr. Hannah Leclerc at UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital here in Longmont again last week. She reminded me that if the PT and injections stop helping, we may eventually have to talk about a lumbar microdiscectomy. I’m hoping it doesn’t come to that, but I also don’t want to ignore it the way I did in 2022.

On a lighter note, I’m planning to come up Sunday for dinner if that still works. I can bring salad and maybe that lemon cake you like. Tell Dad I’ll help him with the backyard fence after we eat as long as he promises not to lecture me about wearing a back brace.

Love you lots,
Emily

P.S. If you need help opening jars or carrying laundry baskets, CALL ME. Don’t be a hero with those wrists.

Title: Random work/ethics brain dump

I’ve been thinking a lot about where my lines are.

March 2022 keeps popping up in my head — that tense meeting in Karen Douglas’s office at High Plains Stables near Loveland. Sitting in that tiny office with the dusty show ribbons on the wall while she accused me of “poaching” two training clients. I remember her saying maybe it was “time to think about moving your operation out by the end of the summer.” I walked out shaking and ended up negotiating that handwritten month-to-month exit agreement just to avoid lawyers and more gossip. I’m still glad I left by July 2022, even if it was messy.

Then there’s the farrier situation from September 2022 at The Ranch in Loveland. Late-night clinic, everyone exhausted, and finding out Daniel Ruiz had been drinking before shoeing a client’s horse earlier that week. Breaking up with him in the parking lot and saying, “I can’t date someone who shows up drunk under a horse,” felt brutal but necessary. Quietly stopped recommending him after that. Still feel weird when his name comes up at barns.

And now, since January 2024, I’ve been driving down to Tom and Erin Wallace’s private place outside Berthoud for those cash-only “emergency tune-up” sessions with their dangerous gelding. No invoices through Cartwright Horsemanship, LLC, no paper trail, just gas money and some extra cash when the schedule is tight. It started as a one-off favor and turned into a semi-regular thing.

I know unreported income is sketchy, and if anything ever happened out there my insurance might not cover it. But also, those sessions have probably prevented someone from getting seriously hurt.

Need to decide:
- Talk to my accountant before tax time about how to clean this up.
- Set a hard boundary with Tom and Erin: either on the books or I’m out.
- Remember that past drama with Karen and Dan is proof that ignoring red flags never ends well.

Event: UCHealth Longs Peak – spine injection follow-up

Date: 2024-04-11
Time: 10:15 a.m. – 11:30 a.m.
Location: UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital, Longmont – Sports Medicine clinic (Dr. Hannah Leclerc)

Notes:
- Follow-up after yesterday’s lumbar injection for chronic lower back pain + right leg numbness.
- Ask Dr. Leclerc again about what signs would mean we’re really heading toward a lumbar microdiscectomy vs. just managing with PT and the occasional shot.
- Remind her that I still rode three horses the day after the last spinal injection (probably not my best life choice) and that I’m trying to be more realistic about my workload.
- No broncs, no first rides on anything questionable this week — “back doc at Longs Peak says no broncs for a week,” as I joked in that email to Laura.
- Block out the afternoon for light groundwork only + admin (invoices, social posts, scheduling).

Other stuff to keep in mind today:
- Call Mom to see how her hands are doing after her last RA check at the UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic in Fort Collins.
- Text Jake about maybe carpooling to Sunday dinner so I’m not driving back from Fort Collins late alone.
- Double-check hay delivery for Friday so I’m not moving bales right after an appointment.

metadata.title: Spine follow-up – Dr. Leclerc
metadata.location: UCHealth Longs Peak Hospital, Longmont CO

artifacts.content:
Hi Sarah,

Thank you again for all the work you’re putting into the 2025 Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge here in Longmont. I know coordinating youth and adult competitors, plus all the mustangs, is a huge job.

I wanted to follow up on the email I sent you back in January from the Longmont Humane Society parking lot — the one where I asked, “Is it possible to withdraw late if my mustang comes in hotter than expected? I’m still a little shaken from a trailer wreck I helped with in Weld County last winter.” I really appreciated how kind your response was.

Since then I’ve been doing a lot of work on my end. I’m still attending that small women-only anxiety support group with therapist Megan Ortiz, LCSW, at the Longmont Community Counseling Center. We’ve talked through the multi-horse trailer rollover on County Road 49 in December 2023 and the earlier trailer-loading accidents I witnessed in 2020 and 2022. Between that and some breathing/grounding tools, I feel more prepared to handle a “hotter” mustang if that’s what I draw.

I also wanted to reassure you that my facility setup is still very much focused on safety: 6-foot pipe-and-rail perimeter, the 50-foot round pen with good footing, and the 20x20 catch pen attached to a sheltered area for the first few days. I’m keeping my schedule realistic so I’m not tempted to rush.

All that to say: I’m planning to stay in unless something truly out of the ordinary happens with the horse’s health or mine. I just thought it was fair to update you, since I had reached out in a moment of doubt.

Please let me know if there’s anything else you need from me logistically as we get closer.

Best,
Emily Cartwright
Cartwright Horsemanship, LLC
Longmont, CO
(970) 555-2843

metadata.subject: TIP Challenge – quick update from my end

artifacts.content:
Laura: You alive? Haven’t seen you in my arena in a week.

Emily: Barely, lol. Long week.

Laura: Horses or humans?

Emily: Both. Mom had another RA monitoring appointment at the UCHealth Rheumatology Clinic in Fort Collins, so I bailed on Wednesday afternoon lessons to drive her. Dr. Natarajan tweaked her methotrexate dose again. She was wiped.

Laura: Ugh. How’s your back with all the extra driving?

Emily: Meh. Saw Dr. Leclerc at UCHealth Longs Peak last month. Same talk: if the PT + injections stop working, we might be looking at a lumbar microdiscectomy eventually. Trying not to think that far ahead.

Laura: You, not overthinking? I’ll believe it when I see it.

Emily: Ha. 

Emily: Money stress isn’t helping either. Still paying off that CareCredit from Copper’s colic surgery at CSU’s vet teaching hospital in Oct 2020. Some months it feels like I’m choosing between my own appointments and hay.

Laura: I hate that for you.

Emily: And I keep hearing Jake in my head from that Sunday dinner in Fort Collins last May: “You can’t keep living like a broke 20-year-old wrangling mustangs forever.” 🙄

Laura: Brothers are the worst.

Emily: On the plus side, Tom and Erin’s demon gelding outside Berthoud is finally leading like a citizen. Those late-night cash-only “emergency tune-ups” might actually keep someone from getting killed.

Laura: You know I’m going to nag you about the off-the-books part, right?

Emily: I know. Accountant first, lecture second. Promise.

Laura: Deal. Come haul in Saturday? I’ll trade arena time for coffee.

Emily: Sold.

Hi Laura,

I hope this email finds you well! I've been meaning to catch up and share some updates, especially with the Colorado Front Range TIP Youth & Adult Challenge coming up. It's hard to believe we've known each other for just over seven years now since I first brought Copper's Comet to your facility. Time sure flies!

Things have been busy here in Longmont. The transition to the new property northeast of town has been smoother than expected, and it's been a blessing to have a more dedicated space for the mustangs and client horses. The facilities are simple but effective, and the horses seem to appreciate the quiet environment.

I've been thinking about that young mustang gelding you watched me work with last winter—the one that was nervous about vehicles. Your advice was invaluable, and breaking the process down into small steps really helped him. It's a method I've refined over the years, and now, as I prepare for the TIP Challenge, those skills will be crucial.

I've also been keeping an eye on my own well-being—my back has been acting up a bit after those long days in the saddle. The yoga classes downtown on Thursdays are helping, and I've added some stretches to my routine at the barn. Speaking of health, I was out chipping ice from the water tanks during that cold snap in January, and it reminded me of the importance of maintaining clean, full water sources for the horses.

Your mentorship and friendship have been invaluable, and I often recommend you to clients seeking advice or a second opinion. I always mention your phone numbers, (303) 555-9174 and (303) 555-6028, as well as your email. I hope you don't mind!

Looking forward to seeing you soon, perhaps at the next clinic or when I haul in for a lesson. Thanks again for all your support and guidance over the years. Your insights always help me refine my approach to training.

Take care,
Emily

metadata.subject: Catching Up and TIP Challenge Preparations
